highIncome.label: High-income city
highIncome.tagline: Affluent residents generate heavy waste, but your treasury and markets can fund high-tech recovery — if you can keep public buy-in.

lowIncome.label: Low / middle-income city
lowIncome.tagline: Fast-growing population, thin budgets, and informal pickers — cheap dumps are tempting, but uncollected waste and disease hit harder here.

worldEvents.name: Recyclables Market Crash
worldEvents.lectureHook: Global recycling economics — when China stopped importing recyclables, many cities lost revenue overnight.

worldEvents.name: National Carbon / Landfill Tax
worldEvents.lectureHook: Denmark's Polluter-Pays principle — landfill and incineration fees rise sharply.

worldEvents.name: Consumption Surge (COVID-style)
worldEvents.lectureHook: Singapore saw +1,334 tonnes of plastic waste during lockdown — your city faces a sudden waste spike.

worldEvents.name: Landfill Fire
worldEvents.lectureHook: Deonar, Jan 2016 — a 3-month fire blanketed Mumbai; nearby life expectancy is 39 vs the state's 73.5.

worldEvents.name: Heatwave & Drought
worldEvents.lectureHook: Ecological deficit — high footprint cities face water stress and biocapacity collapse.

strategyCards.name: Source Separation (Copenhagen)
strategyCards.explainer: Households sort waste at source — high circularity but expensive infrastructure and slow citizen buy-in.

strategyCards.name: Green Exchange (Curitiba)
strategyCards.explainer: Trade recyclables for bus tokens — boosts participation and liveability while cutting waste.

strategyCards.name: Integrate Informal Recyclers (Cairo)
strategyCards.explainer: Formalise zabbaleen scavengers into safe sorting — cheap circularity boost for low-income cities.

strategyCards.name: Waste-to-Energy Plant
strategyCards.explainer: Recover energy from waste — frees landfill space and earns revenue, but pollution and NIMBY risk remain.

strategyCards.name: Packaging Tax
strategyCards.explainer: Tax single-use packaging to cut waste at source — effective long-term but angers businesses and consumers now.

strategyCards.name: Awareness Campaign
strategyCards.explainer: Educate citizens on reduce/reuse — slow payoff but raises participation for future recycling.

strategyCards.name: Repair-and-Reuse Hubs
strategyCards.explainer: Community repair centres extend product life — capped by how many citizens actually participate.

strategyCards.name: Deposit-Return Scheme
strategyCards.explainer: Refundable deposits on bottles and containers — proven reuse boost where collection works.

strategyCards.name: Build Incinerator (Singapore/Edmonton)
strategyCards.explainer: Cuts landfill volume 80–90% and earns energy income — but dioxins, ash, and fierce NIMBY opposition.

strategyCards.name: Engineered Landfill (Semakau)
strategyCards.explainer: Singapore's offshore landfill — safer than open dumps but finite, with slow leachate and methane risks.

strategyCards.name: Open Dumping (Deonar)
strategyCards.explainer: Cheapest disposal — instant capacity relief but severe health, fire, and pollution consequences.

strategyCards.name: Expand Waste Collection
strategyCards.explainer: More trucks and bins — improves liveability but doesn't solve the disposal problem.

strategyCards.name: Polluter-Pays Tax (Denmark)
strategyCards.explainer: Charge producers for waste they create — strong circular incentive but politically costly.

strategyCards.name: Maintain Status Quo
strategyCards.explainer: Save budget this round — but waste keeps piling up and pillars slowly decay.

scenarios.title: The Growing City
scenarios.brief: Your city is booming. Population and affluence are rising fast — and so is the waste stream. How will you set your city's default metabolism?
scenarios.caseFact: Affluent cities generate 2–3× more waste per capita than low-income cities. Linear metabolism treats waste as rubbish; circular metabolism treats it as a resource.
scenarios.decisionPrompt: Invest early in source-separation (costly, slow) or expand collection & dump (cheap)?

quiz.question: Why is 'reduce' at the top of the waste hierarchy?
options: It cuts waste before it is created, avoiding all downstream costs
It is the cheapest option for cities
It generates the most energy from waste

scenarios.title: Running Out of Room
scenarios.brief: Your landfill is filling up fast. Waste hinterlands are under pressure — Semakau is nearly full; Deonar shows what happens when dumps overflow.
scenarios.caseFact: Deonar takes 3,500 t/day; nearby life expectancy is 39 vs the state's 73.5. Semakau was engineered as Singapore's last landfill.
scenarios.decisionPrompt: Build an engineered landfill, keep open-dumping, or cut reliance via recycling?

quiz.question: What is the main risk of relying on open dumps like Deonar?
options: They are too expensive to operate
Severe health hazards, fires, and environmental contamination
They require too much citizen participation
They produce too much recyclable material

scenarios.title: Waste to Energy?
scenarios.brief: Residents resist a proposed incinerator near their homes. NIMBY protests are fierce — but landfill space is critical.
scenarios.caseFact: Singapore and Edmonton use incineration to cut landfill volume 80–90% and recover energy — but dioxins and ash remain concerns.
scenarios.decisionPrompt: Build the incinerator or find another way to manage growing waste?

quiz.question: What is a key trade-off of incineration?
options: It frees landfill space and earns energy but harms air quality and liveability
It is always cheaper than recycling
It eliminates the need for any waste collection

scenarios.title: The Footprint Bill
scenarios.brief: Your city's ecological footprint now exceeds local biocapacity — an ecological deficit. Resources are being absorbed from distant hinterlands.
scenarios.caseFact: High consumption cities import food, materials, and energy from global hinterlands, creating invisible environmental damage elsewhere.
scenarios.decisionPrompt: Cut consumption at source (tax/reduce) or keep importing and emitting?

quiz.question: What drives a city's ecological footprint?
options: Population, affluence, and consumption patterns absorbing resources from hinterlands

scenarios.title: People & Livelihoods
scenarios.brief: Informal scavengers recycle a large share of your waste — but unsafely, without protection or fair pay.
scenarios.caseFact: Cairo's zabbaleen recycle up to 80% of the city's waste informally. Integrating them formally boosted safety and circularity.
scenarios.decisionPrompt: Integrate informal recyclers formally, ignore them, or evict them?

quiz.question: Why integrate informal recyclers rather than evict them?
options: They cost the city more money
They already provide cheap circularity — formalisation improves safety and liveability

scenarios.title: Zero-Waste Vision
scenarios.brief: Final round — choose your flagship circular city policy. This locks in your city's long-term trajectory.
scenarios.caseFact: Copenhagen targets zero waste; Curitiba pioneered the Green Exchange; Denmark leads with Polluter-Pays legislation.
scenarios.decisionPrompt: Polluter-Pays tax, Green Exchange incentive, or full circular source-separation?

quiz.question: Why can the 'most sustainable' strategy still lose in this game?
options: Escalating costs, participation limits, and market volatility can bankrupt a max-recycle city
